course_description: This course introduces students to the Australian legal system and develops student knowledge of Australian business and corporations law. The course is intended to provide a foundation for non-legal professionals intending to work in business.

requirements.restrictions: Course is not recommended for students with legal qualifications
requirements.incompatible: (LAWS1100 + LAWS3100) or LAWS4112 or LAWS5017 or (LAWS7000 + LAWS7011) or (LAWS7010 + LAWS7011) or LAWS7117 or LAWS7234 or LAWS7317 Not available to students in the LLM, LLM(Adv), LLM(#16) or LLM(#24) programs
requirements.assumed_background: No assumed background in legal studies is required. This course is not recommended for students with legal qualifications.

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Additional Timetable Information
Please enrol for a seminar that best fits in with your commitments as soon as possible.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The broad aim of LAWS7023 is to give you sufficient knowledge and understanding of Australian business and corporate law to recognise, resolve and avoid legal problems in your business, commercial and corporate dealings.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an acceptable level of knowledge and understanding of the elements of Australian business and corporate law.
Communicate your knowledge of Australian business and corporate law effectively and appropriately, in writing and verbally, to your instructors and to your fellow students.
Navigate independently through legal information environments, including online legal resources.
Use your knowledge of Australian business and corporate law to identify, analyse and solve simple legal problems.
Appreciate your legal, social and professional responsibilities to your peers, your profession and your community.

assessment_summary.title: Corporate Law Multiple Choice Question Quiz

assessment_details.title: Corporate Law Final Assessment
assessment_details.task_description:
This assessment will occur during the UQ Final Exam Period and students will have 48 hours to complete it. Exact details will be provided on Blackboard.

The 40% Corporations Law Final Assessment will be open book. It will consist of two (2) short answer problem solving questions and one (1) ILAC problem solving question. All corporate law topics may be assessed.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

Submission:

Assignments must be submitted electronically in both Word and PDF via the online assessment submission link on the course Blackboard site.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Problem Questions
assessment_details.task_description:
This assessment task requires submission of a one-to-two-page (A4) answer to the Assessable Tutorial Problem Questions scheduled for a Seminar during the semester. Each of the 7 tutorial questions is worth 2 marks each (2% of the overall course mark) and 2 marks will be allocated for the identification and treatment of the issues relevant to the solution of each tutorial problem question.

This means that students can submit 5 out of 7 answers and still receive full marks for this assessment item. Students can submit answers for all 7 tutorial problem questions if they wish and their final marks will be calculated on their best 5 submissions to a limit of 10% worth of marks.

The Tutorial Problem Questions will be short answer questions and students must make a serious and reasonable attempt to answer a Tutorial Problem question.

Please take note of the following:

The answer for each Tutorial Problem Question will be discussed in the relevant Tutorial itself. Tutorials are not recorded and whilst dot points about the answer to each tutorial problem question will be released at the end of each week, these dot points will not be a full and formative answer to a Tutorial Problem Question - students are encouraged to attend the Tutorials each week to obtain the full benefit of the discussion since this will aid them in their understanding of both the subject material and short answer and ILAC question technique;

At the end of the semester, a mark out of 10 will be given to each student if, in the overall judgement of the course coordinator, the criteria set out in the ECP have been satisfied.

Answers cannot be submitted after the submission due time for each Tutorial Problem Question - whether to the submission folder or by email - see Announcements and the Learning Resources page in Blackboard for due times for the submission of each assessable Tutorial Problem Question.

The ability for students to submit 5 out of 7 answers and still receive full marks for this assessment item is designed to accommodate for student work commitments, emergencies and other reasons for non-submission.

Note: Student answers to Tutorial Problem Questions must be typed and must be submitted electronically in Word in the relevant submission folder in the course Blackboard site. Students only need to submit their final draft for submission for the Tutorial Problem Questions.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: ILAC Submission
assessment_details.task_description:
Students are required to submit an ILAC answer to a formative assessable ILAC problem question in Teaching Week 5 - group feedback will be provided.

This formative assessment will require both typed comments and a typed answer to an Assessable 15% ILAC problem question which will be due online in Teaching Week 5 at 2pm on Thursday 26 August. Submissions received after 2pm Thursday 26 August will be submitted into the Late Submissions folder and will incur Late Penalties - see paragraph 5.3 of the ECP regarding late submissions of assessment.

The Assessable 15% ILAC submission must be a minimum of 4-5 typed A4 sized pages - students are encouraged to write more if they wish. Your written answers must be submitted to an online assessment folder (Details to be announced on Blackboard) and cannot be submitted by email or otherwise.

Students will be required to submit 3 drafts of their 15% ILAC submission for examination - being their Research Draft, their Second ILAC Draft and their Final Submission Draft - only the Final Submission draft will be assessed.

Group Feedback will be provided to the Assessable 15% ILAC submission.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:

or

Submission:

Assignments must be submitted electronically in both Word and PDF via the online assessment submission link on the course Blackboard site.

assessment_details.title: Business Law Assignment
assessment_details.task_description:
A 25% assignment containing one ILAC problem question and based on Business Law. Any of the Business Law topics from the seminars and the tutorials may be assessed in the assignment.

The assignment question will be released in Teaching Week 7 and it will be due for submission online in the 25% Business Law Assignment folder in the Assessments page on Blackboard at 2pm on Thursday 16 September 2021 in Teaching Week 8. Assignments submitted after 2pm Thursday 16 September 2021 will be submitted into the Late Submissions folder and will incur Late Penalties - see paragraph 5.3 of the ECP regarding late submissions of assessment.

Word Limit 3,500 words. Anything beyond 3,500 words will not be marked (Not including Assignment cover sheet and footnotes). Your written answers must be submitted in the submission folder and cannot be submitted by email or otherwise. Group Feedback will be provided to the Assignment.

Students will be required to submit 3 drafts of their assignment - being their Research D raft, their Second ILAC Draft and their Final Submission Draft - only the Final Submission Draft will be assessed.

assessment_details.task_description: The 10% Corporate Law Multiple Choice Exam will be a 30 minute multiple choice online exam to be held during Teaching Week 12. It consists of ten (10) multiple choice problem questions worth one mark each. All Corporations Law topics may be assessed.

learning_resources.required_resources: Material published via the Course Blackboard Site
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS7023 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Mon - Fri Principles of Law and the Australian Legal System : Teaching Week 1 Seminar: Starting in Teaching Week 1 and continuing through to Teaching Week 12, there will be weekly seminars which all students must attend - internal students will attend in person and external students will attend online via zoom. This seminar will discuss how to do well in this subject, an introduction to Australia's legal system, common law and legislation and a short introduction on how to answer a legal problem question using the ILAC method (Issue, Law, Application and Conclusion)- which will be discussed at length in the Teaching Week 1 seminars. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Tutorials (Non-Assessable) : Starting in Teaching Week 1 and continuing through to Teaching Week 12, there will be weekly tutorials which all students must attend - there will be a weekly tutorials for internal students and weekly online tutorials for external students to discuss tutorial problem questions and student queries arising from the course content. In teaching week 1 the tutorial will be non-assessible and will involve welcoming students to the course, how to do well in this course including the importance of summarising the subject material on a weekly basis and the three draft strategy, a discussion of a non-assessable ILAC question which students must submit online by 2pm Monday 2 August in Teaching Week 2 and a Visual Workshop on How Students can write answers to a legal problem question called an ILAC Question (Issue, Law, Application, Conclusion). Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Mon - Fri Formation of Contracts - Contracts 1 : Teaching Week 2 Seminar: This Seminar will introduce the basic concepts of contract law including, offer, acceptance, and consideration. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Tutorials : Non-assessable ILAC question due online 2pm Monday 2 August - Discussion of non-assessable ILAC Question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on next weeks tutorial question and a discussion of short answer question technique. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Enforcing Contracts - Contracts 2 : Teaching Week 3 Seminar: Note: Wednesday 11 August is a Public Holiday - No seminar class. This seminar will instead be recorded and made available to students. This seminar will examine when a contract is enforceable and when it is invalid and unenforceable. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Tutorials : Assessable 10% Seminars begin - 2 marks each - Discussion of seminar question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming assessable tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri 15% ILAC Submission Released (Assessable) : Students will have to prepare a typed commentary on a pretend students ILAC answer and students will have to prepare their own typed written ILAC answer to an Assessable ILAC question and submit both items to Blackboard by 2pm Thursday 26 August in Teaching Week 5. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Causing Harm - Torts : Teaching Week 4 Seminar: This seminar will examine the basic concepts of tort law including negligence, duty of care, breach of duty of care and causation. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Consumers and Anti-Competitive Behaviour Law : Teaching Week 5 Seminar: This seminar will examine consumer law in Australia, in particular the consumer protection provisions of the Competition and Consumer Act as well as examining the Anti-competitive behaviour provisions. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Sun Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Sun 15% ILAC Submission Due (Assessable) : 15% ILAC Submission - Due online on Blackboard in Teaching Week 5 by 2pm Thursday 26 August 2021.

Mon - Fri Intellectual Property : Teaching Week 6 Seminar: This seminar will look at Intellectual Property (IP) Law, being the set of rules that protect IP from unauthorised use and exploitation by others. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Entities for Conducting Business : Teaching Week 7 Seminar: This seminar will introduce organisations for conducting business including partnerships and companies, including setting up companies, classification, types and status of companies and the company registration process. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Sun Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Sun 25% Business Law Assignment Question Released (Assessable) : 25% Business Law Assignment - Released in Teaching Week 7 - Due online on Blackboard in Teaching Week 8 by 2pm Thursday 16 September 2021. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Finance and Corporate Governance : Teaching Week 8 Seminar: This seminar will cover various methods of raising corporate finance and issues of corporate governance including internal governance rules, corporate liability, members, members decision-making, members powers and members remedies. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Mon - Sun 25% Business Law Assignment Due (Assessable) : 25% Business Law Assignment - Due online on Blackboard in Teaching Week 8 by 2pm Thursday 16 September 2021.

Mon - Fri Directors Duties (1) : Teaching Week 9 Seminar: This seminar will cover issues of directorship including the role of directors, directors decision-making powers, an overview of director's duties and their function and the director's duty of care. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Mid-Semester Break : No Seminars or Tutorials
Mon - Sat Mid-Semester Break : No Seminars or Tutorials

Mon - Fri Directors Duties (2) : Teaching Week 10 Seminar: This seminar will cover the second part of directors duties including the duty to retain discretions, the duty to act in good faith in the best interests of the company, the duty to act for a proper purpose, the duty to avoid conflicts of interest, breach of duty and remedies. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Mon - Fri Members Rights, Remedies and Insolvency : Teaching Week 11 Seminar: This seminar will cover methods of protecting members' rights and the ways in which members can claim damage when their rights are affected. It also covers issues of external administration and liquidation. Students must read the seminar material before attending the seminar, then listen to the seminar recording and summarise the seminar and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content- release and guidance on coming tutorial questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Final Assessment Preparation : Teaching Week 12 Seminar Recording: This seminar recording will discuss the Final Corporate Law Assessment and provides non-assessable practice questions to help students learn how to write answers to short answer and ILAC final assessment questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Tutorials : Discussion of tutorial question, quizzes, subject content - discussion of practice assessment questions. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Sun 10% Corporations Law Multiple-Choice Quiz (Assessable) : Open Book - 10 Multiple-Choice Problem Questions - This 30 minute online quiz will take place during TW12 - further details to be advised. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Mon - Fri Consultation Week : No seminars are scheduled in this week to assist students in their study leading up to the UQ Final Exam Period - face to face and online consultation times will be held at the same time as the regular seminar times to help students with their queries about the subject material in the lead up to the 40% Final Corporate Law Assessment - and the non-assessable practice exams questions released in TW12 will be discussed in these consultations. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard
Mon - Fri Revision Week
Mon - Fri UQ Final Exam Period
Mon - Fri 40% Final Corporate Law Assessment (Assessable) : To be held over the UQ Final Exam Period- Further details to be advised. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with Section 3.4 of PPL3.10.02 Assessment ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 3.8 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Seminars

Seminars are a forum where the lecturer can:

Explore theoretical and legal concepts relevant to the topic;

Critically evaluate these concepts and explore their relationships to one another;

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration;

Place the material discussed into a wider intellectual and practical context.

Seminars afford students an opportunity in understanding the subject content with a view to developing their skills in analytical reasoning and argument. As an interactive learning forum, the seminar is dependent upon all students having read the relevant lecture material before attending the seminar and being prepared to discuss lecture content. Students must read the lecture material before attending seminars and be prepared to actively take notes about what the lecturer says in the seminar.

The Function of Tutorials

Tutorials offer students an interactive forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures and to engage with the course coordinator and other students in exploring those issues. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

As an interactive learning forum, the tutorial is dependent upon all students having prepared the material to be considered and being prepared to think about the concepts raised, ask questions and engage in discussion.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% +

Other Requirements & Comments:

Please Note: There is a STRONG correlation between seminar attendance and assessment performance - All students are strongly encouraged to ensure that they attend EACH and EVERY seminar.
late_submission:
Penalties for Late Submission

Where a non-examination item of assessment (eg essays, assignments etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day that the assessment is overdue. The date and time of submission will be deemed to be that noted on the electronic submission. Assessment submitted more than 7 days after the due date and time will receive zero marks.

Example : Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on Monday, assessment submitted after 2pm on Monday but before 2pm on Tuesday will receive a 10% penalty.

Example : Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on the 10th, any work submitted after 2pm on the 17th will receive zero marks.

Extension to Assessment Due Date

Extensions will not normally exceed 14 days. If you have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 14 days, we advise you to carefully consider whether you are capable of successfully completing your courses this semester. You might be eligible to withdraw without academic penalty.

For full details, students should refer to the Guidelines for Applying for an Extension . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for assessment will only be considered in respect of a certified medical condition . Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment . Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date must include evidence of the reasons for the late request. Late requests received by the School will require the Deputy Director, Academic Services to accept the request for consideration.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.
